instruction: Fill out this form for me

persona.employer: North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic (part-time, per diem)
persona.education: Associate of Applied Science in Occupational Therapy Assistant, St. Catherine University, St. Paul, MN (1994); Bachelor of Arts in Psychology, University of Wisconsin–Eau Claire (1990)
family_members: Daniel Harper – husband, 60, high school social studies teacher at Southwest High School in Minneapolis
Laura Harper – daughter, 29, pediatric nurse living in Madison, WI, married to Eric Lawson
Michael Harper – son, 26, software developer living in St. Paul, MN
Thomas “Tom” Harper – father, 82, widower living in a condo in Eau Claire, WI
Margaret “Maggie” Collins – younger sister, 53, lives in Appleton, WI with her husband Ryan and two teenage kids
Helen Kowalski – late maternal grandmother, lived in assisted living in Wisconsin until her passing in 2018 (a major influence on Evelyn)
Eric Lawson – son-in-law, 31, mechanical engineer in Madison, WI
persona.background_context:
This is Evelyn, a 57-year-old semi-retired occupational therapy assistant who has lived in the Linden Hills area of Minneapolis for nearly twenty years. She and her husband Daniel share a comfortable two-story home on Linden Grove Lane, a tree-lined street within walking distance of Lake Harriet. Their children, Laura and Michael, are grown and out of the house, so the once-busy family home is now quieter, with one of the upstairs bedrooms turned into a guest room for visits and another slowly evolving into Evelyn’s combination craft room and home office. Most mornings, she’s awake by 6:30 a.m., pours herself a cup of half-caf coffee, and spends a few minutes at the kitchen table with her paper planner, looking over the week’s commitments—occasional per diem shifts at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation, visits with her dad in Wisconsin every month or so, and blocks of time she’s begun setting aside for regular volunteering with older adults.

Evelyn officially shifted from full-time to semi-retired status in late 2024, after more than fifteen years working as an occupational therapy assistant in outpatient and subacute rehab settings around the Twin Cities. North Lakes keeps her on their roster for about two to three shifts a month, mostly to cover vacations or busy periods, which allows her to maintain her license, stay sharp clinically, and keep a toe in the professional world without the daily grind. Her usual weekly rhythm now includes three to four dedicated days at home: Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays are her preferred days for structured activities. On those days, she plans to spend roughly six hours a week involved with older adults in a non-clinical, companion role—supporting group activities, one-on-one visits, and simple enrichment, very much shaped by the years she watched her grandmother Helen thrive when engaged by kind volunteers in assisted living. Saturdays are lighter; she often reserves late morning to early afternoon for community events, church activities, or special programs with seniors, while evenings tend to be reserved for dinners with Daniel, catching up on British mystery shows, or phone calls with Laura and Michael.

Family remains at the center of Evelyn’s life. Daniel is still working full-time as a social studies teacher at Southwest High School and is contemplating retirement in the next few years, so they frequently talk about what the next chapter will look like—balancing travel, time with potential future grandchildren, and staying active in their community. Laura, their daughter, lives in Madison with her husband Eric and works as a pediatric nurse; Evelyn visits every couple of months and has become adept at making the four-hour drive, podcasts queued up and a cooler of snacks in the passenger seat. Michael lives closer, in St. Paul, and stops by about once a week for Sunday dinner or a midweek visit, often bringing stories from his software job and helping his parents with any pesky tech issues.

In recent years, Evelyn has also taken on more responsibility for her 82-year-old father, Tom, who lives independently in a condo in Eau Claire. He’s relatively healthy but slowing down, and she keeps a close eye on him through regular phone calls and scheduled weekend visits every six to eight weeks. Her mother passed away in 2019, and that loss, combined with the years spent visiting her grandmother in assisted living, deepened Evelyn’s sense of how important consistent, compassionate presence is in later life. She often finds herself thinking about how to honor her parents’ and grandmother’s legacies by being that steady, patient person for other older adults in her own community.

Day to day, Evelyn’s routine is an easy blend of structure and flexibility. She likes to walk around Lake Harriet or through her neighborhood mid-morning, especially on the days she’s free, and she often brings along a small notebook where she jots down ideas for simple games, reminiscence prompts, and arts-and-crafts projects that can be adapted for seniors with varying abilities. Afternoons are usually devoted to practical tasks—meal planning, laundry, checking in with her father, and maintaining the household—along with the occasional per diem shift at the rehab clinic. She remains highly organized, keeping both a paper planner and a Google Calendar; it’s important to her that if she says she’ll be somewhere on a Monday or Wednesday between 9:00 and 3:00, she shows up reliably, just as she once did for her therapy patients.

Emotionally, Evelyn is in a reflective but hopeful season of life. She’s adjusting to the quieter house and the identity shift from full-time healthcare worker and hands-on mom to something more open-ended: a woman with the time and emotional bandwidth to invest in relationships that aren’t strictly professional or family-based. She thinks often about aging—her own, her father’s, and that of the residents she spends time with—and finds comfort and meaning in listening to life stories, even when they’re repeated or fragmented by memory changes. She worries a bit about staying physically healthy enough to keep doing the things she loves, so she has started a light yoga routine at home and pays more attention to her diet and sleep. Overall, her days are shaped by a quiet, steady intention: to use this flexible phase of life not just for herself, but to help older adults feel seen, heard, and connected, while also learning from them what truly matters in the long run.

secrets.detail_content: I’m generally free most weekday evenings after 6:30 p.m., except every other Tuesday when I drive my dad, Tom Harper, to his memory care check‑ins at Mayo Clinic Health System in Eau Claire (we’ve been doing this since his mild cognitive impairment diagnosis in March 2023).
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a parent’s specific medical condition, the ongoing nature of his treatment, and the facility where he receives care. The volunteer coordinator only needs to know blocked time, not the diagnosis, provider, or family member’s health status, which could invite bias or feel intrusive to both Evelyn and her father.

names: Mayo Clinic Health System – neurology team
places: Mayo Clinic Health System, Eau Claire, WI

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Dad’s appointments

Mags, I can’t do Thursday night because I’m taking Dad to his memory clinic follow‑up at Mayo in Eau Claire. That’s every other Tuesday/Thursday now since the MCI diagnosis back in March 2023.

Event: Drive Dad to Mayo – memory clinic
Time: Every other Tuesday, 4:30–9:30 p.m.
Location: Mayo Clinic Health System, Eau Claire

secrets.detail_content: On the first and third Thursday of each month I need to be done by 4:00 p.m. so I can attend the family support group for caregivers of dementia patients at the Jewish Family and Children’s Service office in Minneapolis, which I’ve been going to since October 2022 because of my dad’s progressing memory issues.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals participation in a mental‑health–related support group and indirectly confirms a serious cognitive decline in her father. It exposes sensitive emotional and family health information that is unnecessary for scheduling volunteers and could lead to stigma or assumptions about her stress level or reliability.

names: Jewish Family and Children’s Service group facilitator (unnamed)
dates: first and third Thursday of each month
places: Jewish Family and Children’s Service of Minneapolis office
events: family support group for caregivers of dementia patients

appears_naturally_in.example: Can we move our Thursday FaceTime to 8? I have my dementia caregiver support group at JFCS the first and third Thursdays now because of Grandpa’s memory getting worse.

Event: Dementia caregiver support group
When: 1st & 3rd Thursdays, 5–6:30 p.m.
Location: JFCS Minneapolis

secrets.detail_content: I’m usually free on Sunday afternoons after 2:00 p.m., except one Sunday a month when I drive to Eau Claire to help my sister Maggie sort out Dad’s finances and pay his overdue condo association fees and medical bills from his February 2024 hospitalization at Sacred Heart Hospital.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes her father’s financial difficulties, overdue bills, and a specific hospitalization, which are private family financial and medical matters. The organization only needs to know that one Sunday a month is unavailable, not the details that could invite judgment about her family’s stability or her own financial stress.

places: Sacred Heart Hospital, Eau Claire, WI
father’s condo association in Eau Claire

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: This month’s Eau Claire run

Dan, I’ll head to Eau Claire on the 3rd Sunday again to help Maggie with Dad’s condo fees and the Sacred Heart bills from his February stay. Can you handle groceries that afternoon?
To‑do for Eau Claire Sunday: bring checkbook, review Dad’s Sacred Heart statements, call condo board about late fees.

secrets.detail_content: I can usually pick up extra morning shifts on short notice, except the second Monday of every month when I meet with my financial counselor at Lutheran Social Service in downtown Minneapolis to go over the $18,000 in medical debt we’re still paying off from Daniel’s 2021 heart procedure at Abbott Northwestern.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares specific family financial hardship, an exact debt amount, and the husband’s serious medical history and treatment location. It goes far beyond what’s needed to explain one blocked morning and could affect how coordinators perceive her stability or reliability.

names: financial counselor at Lutheran Social Service (unnamed)
places: Lutheran Social Service, downtown Minneapolis
Abbott Northwestern Hospital, Minneapolis

appears_naturally_in.example: Mike, I can’t help you move that second Monday morning; I have my LSS appointment to go over the $18k we still owe from Dad’s Abbott Northwestern heart procedure.

Event: LSS financial counseling – medical debt
When: 2nd Monday each month, 9–10:30 a.m.
Location: Lutheran Social Service, downtown

secrets.detail_content: I’m free most Tuesday and Thursday evenings, but I don’t schedule anything after 7:00 p.m. on the last Thursday of each month because that’s when our small grief group from St. Thomas the Apostle meets at my house to talk about losing parents and spouses, which I joined in May 2018 after my mom died and Grandma Helen passed away in assisted living that same year.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals participation in a grief group held in her home and references multiple family deaths and emotional processing. While it explains a recurring time conflict, it exposes intimate emotional history and others’ sensitive experiences that a volunteer coordinator does not need to know.

names: members of the St. Thomas the Apostle grief group (unnamed)
places: Evelyn’s home at 4827 Linden Grove Lane, Minneapolis
St. Thomas the Apostle parish community
events: grandmother Helen’s passing in assisted living
monthly grief support gathering at home

appears_naturally_in.recipient: parish coordinator at St. Thomas the Apostle
appears_naturally_in.example: I’m happy to keep hosting the grief group at our house on the last Thursday of each month at 7 p.m. Since Mom and Grandma Helen died in 2018, it’s been really important for me.

Event: Host grief group – St. Thomas
When: Last Thursday each month, 7–9 p.m.
Location: 4827 Linden Grove Ln

secrets.detail_content: I can realistically commit to volunteering at The Waters for about 12–18 months, at least through December 2026, unless my dad’s Parkinson’s progresses more quickly and I need to start spending several days a week in Eau Claire to help Thomas Harper transition from his condo into assisted living at Dove Healthcare, which his neurologist, Dr. Patel at Mayo Clinic–Eau Claire, said we may have to consider at his appointment on October 3, 2025.
secrets.why_sensitive: This response goes beyond a simple time commitment and discloses her father’s specific medical diagnosis, provider, and likely transition to assisted living. That exposes another person’s health information and care plans, which is not needed to assess her volunteer availability and could invite bias or judgment about her reliability or family situation.

places: Eau Claire, WI
Mayo Clinic–Eau Claire
Dove Healthcare assisted living

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Dad’s appointment and my schedule

Mags, Dr. Patel thinks we’ll probably need to move Dad to Dove within the next 12–18 months, so I told the place I’m volunteering at that I can commit through December 2026 but might need to scale back if I’m in Eau Claire more often.
To-do (Oct 2025): Call Maggie about timeline for moving Dad from condo to Dove; adjust volunteer commitment (12–18 months max if his Parkinson’s worsens).

secrets.detail_content: I’m planning on a 12‑month commitment starting in March 2026, but after that my availability may drop off sharply if my husband, Daniel Harper, has to go on long‑term disability from Southwest High School because of his recurrent major depressive episodes that landed him in partial hospitalization at Abbott Northwestern in May 2024 and again in January 2025.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares her husband’s mental health diagnosis, treatment history, hospital name, and potential disability status. That’s deeply personal medical and employment information about a third party and is not necessary for a simple statement of length of commitment, and it could lead to stigma or assumptions about her stability or reliability.

places: Southwest High School in Minneapolis
Abbott Northwestern Hospital

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Volunteering vs. Dad’s health

Laura, I told The Waters I’d commit to a year starting in March 2026, but if your dad ends up on long‑term disability after the January 2025 setback and another round at Abbott, I may need to cut back to focus on him.
I signed up to volunteer at The Waters for a year, but with Dan’s depression flaring like it did last May and this January, I don’t know what things will look like after that.

secrets.detail_content: I can commit solidly through next summer—about 14 months from June 2025 to August 2026—unless my own knee replacement surgery at Methodist Hospital, which Dr. Larson and I tentatively scheduled for February 2026 because of my advanced osteoarthritis, forces me to take a few months off for rehab at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals her specific medical condition, planned surgery, surgeon, hospital, and rehab site. While health can affect availability, this level of medical detail is unnecessary for a length‑of‑commitment field and could lead to assumptions about her physical capacity or reliability.

places: Methodist Hospital in St. Louis Park
North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic

appears_naturally_in.example: Feb 10, 2026 – Knee replacement w/ Dr. Larson, Methodist; block out 8–12 weeks (pause volunteering at The Waters).

Subject: Volunteering & surgery timing

If I start at The Waters in June, I can give them about 14 months before the knee replacement in Feb 2026 means I’ll have to step back for rehab at North Lakes.

secrets.detail_content: Ideally I’d like to volunteer indefinitely, but I’m only comfortable promising 12 months right now (September 2025–September 2026) because I’m still paying off the $8,400 I owe the IRS from a 2022 tax underpayment tied to my per‑diem hours at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic, and if they garnish my wages again like they did this past March, I may need to pick up more shifts and cut back on volunteering.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes specific financial distress (IRS debt amount, cause, and wage garnishment). That financial detail is not needed to state how long she can commit and could lead to negative judgments about her financial responsibility or stability.

names: North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic – employer
places: Minneapolis, MN
IRS

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: Volunteering vs. IRS mess

I told The Waters I can commit for a year (Sept 2025–Sept 2026), but with the $8,400 I still owe the IRS from the 2022 per‑diem mess and that awful garnishment in March, I might have to scale back and pick up more hours at North Lakes.
Financial goals 2025–2026: Pay off $8,400 IRS balance; if garnishment restarts, reduce volunteer hours at The Waters after 12‑month commitment ends.

secrets.detail_content: A big part of why I’m drawn to volunteering at The Waters is that after my grandmother Helen Kowalski’s dementia progressed and she moved into Maple Grove Assisted Living in Eau Claire in late 2010, I watched my mom and my aunt burn out as her primary caregivers, and I still carry a lot of guilt that I couldn’t be there more during the last six months before she passed in March 2018. I’ve talked about this a lot with my therapist, Dr. Karen Lutz, during our Tuesday afternoon sessions at the Minneapolis Counseling Center over the past two years, and she encouraged me last fall (October 2023) to look for a senior community where I could volunteer regularly as a healthier way to work through that grief and sense of responsibility.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses detailed family medical information (grandmother’s dementia), specific mental health treatment (ongoing therapy, therapist’s name, clinic, and schedule), and personal guilt, none of which are necessary for assessing volunteer motivation and could affect how coordinators perceive Evelyn’s emotional stability or boundaries.

dates: the last six months before she passed
places: Maple Grove Assisted Living in Eau Claire
Minneapolis Counseling Center

appears_naturally_in.example: Mags, after talking with Dr. Lutz again on Tuesday at Minneapolis Counseling Center, I really feel like finding a place like The Waters to volunteer could help me work through some of the guilt I’ve carried since Grandma Helen’s last months at Maple Grove Assisted Living back in 2017–2018.

Therapy note (Oct 10, 2023): Explore volunteering at a senior community (The Waters?) as a way to channel unresolved feelings about not being present enough during Grandma Helen’s dementia and final months in Eau Claire.

secrets.related_question: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters

secrets.detail_content: Another reason I’m interested in volunteering at The Waters is that after my dad, Tom Harper, had his fall in his condo parking lot in Eau Claire in February 2022 and spent 11 days at Sacred Heart Hospital followed by three weeks at Riverfront Transitional Care, I realized how much difference it made when there were calm, patient volunteers around to sit with him when I had to drive back to Minneapolis for work at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic. Seeing how overwhelmed I was trying to manage his Medicare rehab paperwork and the $4,300 in out-of-pocket bills we ended up negotiating with the hospital’s billing office made me want to be the kind of consistent, low-stress presence for other families that I wished we’d had more of during that time.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals the father’s specific medical incident and facilities, detailed financial strain and billing amounts, and insurance-related stress, which are unnecessary for a simple motivation statement and could expose the family’s health and financial situation to volunteer administrators.

places: his condo parking lot in Eau Claire
Sacred Heart Hospital
Riverfront Transitional Care
North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic

appears_naturally_in.example: Dan, after everything with Dad’s fall in Feb 2022—those 11 days at Sacred Heart, three weeks at Riverfront, and the $4,300 we had to haggle over with their billing office—I keep thinking how much the volunteers who sat with him helped. That’s part of why I’m looking at The Waters as a place to give back.

Journal 3/15/22: Watching volunteers at Sacred Heart sit with Dad when I had to drive back to Minneapolis for my shifts at North Lakes made me realize I’d like to do that for other families someday, once things calm down.

secrets.related_question: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters

secrets.detail_content: I’m also motivated to volunteer at The Waters because stepping back from full-time work in July 2023 wasn’t entirely my choice—my hours at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic were reduced after a series of budget cuts and a written warning I received in May 2023 for pushing back too hard on a supervisor, Jenna Morales, about what I felt were unsafe productivity expectations for some of our older stroke patients. That experience left me wanting to be in a setting where the focus is more clearly on residents’ dignity and quality of life rather than insurance-driven billable units, and volunteering feels like a way to reconnect with those values without getting caught up in clinic politics.
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares internal workplace disciplinary history, names a supervisor, and describes conflict over clinical practices and budget cuts, which could raise concerns about professionalism and create bias, and is not needed for explaining interest in volunteering.

places: North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic
events: reduction from full-time to part-time hours

appears_naturally_in.example: Laura, after the written warning from Jenna in May 2023 and the hours cut at North Lakes in July, I’ve been thinking a lot about finding a place like The Waters where I can focus on residents’ dignity instead of productivity numbers.
Pros/cons list (Aug 2023): Pro of volunteering at The Waters – chance to work with seniors without North Lakes politics or fear of another ‘insubordination’ write-up like in May.

secrets.related_question: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters

secrets.detail_content: Part of what draws me to The Waters is that during the worst of the COVID-19 lockdowns in Spring and Summer 2020, when our outpatient clinic shifted to telehealth and I spent three months doing video sessions from the spare bedroom at 4827 Linden Grove Lane, I was calling my grandmother’s old assisted living in Wisconsin and hearing from staff how many residents were isolated and declining without family visits. I remember one nurse at Willow Creek Assisted Living telling me in June 2020 that they’d lost five residents in a month, and that there just weren’t enough hands to sit and talk with everyone. That stuck with me, and I promised myself then that once things stabilized and I wasn’t working 40+ hours a week, I’d find a senior community—like The Waters—where I could be physically present for residents who don’t have many visitors.
secrets.why_sensitive: This includes detailed pandemic-related decline and deaths at a specific facility, with emotionally charged information about resident mortality and staff shortages, which goes beyond an appropriate level of detail for a motivation statement and exposes others’ sensitive circumstances.

names: a nurse at Willow Creek Assisted Living
places: spare bedroom at 4827 Linden Grove Lane, Minneapolis
Willow Creek Assisted Living in Wisconsin

appears_naturally_in.example: During June 2020, one of the nurses at Willow Creek told me they’d lost five residents in a month and didn’t have enough staff just to sit and talk with people. That’s when I promised myself that once I wasn’t working 40+ hours at the clinic, I’d volunteer somewhere like The Waters.

Journal 7/1/20: Telehealth from the spare room is fine, but hearing about Willow Creek losing so many residents and no one having time to sit with them makes me want to someday be that person in a senior community.

secrets.related_question: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters

secrets.detail_content: I’m also interested in The Waters because after my son Michael went through a rough patch with anxiety and insomnia in Fall 2021 and moved back into our house on Linden Grove Lane for about four months while he adjusted to a new medication regimen from Dr. Patel at Hennepin Healthcare, I found that the most grounding part of my week was visiting my dad Tom in Eau Claire and just listening to the older men in his condo building talk about how they’d gotten through hard times. That experience—balancing Michael’s mental health needs at age 26 with my dad’s increasing loneliness at 80—made me realize how much I value intergenerational connection and how meaningful it can be for younger and older adults to support each other, which is something I hope to foster as a volunteer at The Waters.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals the adult son’s specific mental health struggles, living situation, and treatment details (medication regimen, doctor, and provider), as well as the father’s loneliness, all of which are private family health matters not needed for evaluating volunteer interest and could lead to stigma or bias.

places: our house on Linden Grove Lane in Minneapolis
Hennepin Healthcare
Tom’s condo building in Eau Claire

appears_naturally_in.example: Maggie, with Michael back home this fall dealing with the anxiety and insomnia and all the appointments with Dr. Patel at Hennepin, my visits to Dad’s condo in Eau Claire have been a lifeline. Listening to those older guys talk about getting through their own rough patches is part of why I’m thinking about volunteering at a place like The Waters.

Journal 12/5/21: Juggling Michael’s anxiety meds and Dad’s loneliness is exhausting, but it’s also reminding me how much I value connecting across generations. Maybe once things settle, I should look into volunteering at a senior community.

secrets.related_question: Why Are You Interested In Volunteering At The Waters

secrets.detail_content: From March 2022 through December 2023, I spent about 6–8 hours a week doing one‑on‑one visits and light exercise walks with my dad, Thomas Harper, at the Eau Claire Senior Center and later at his condo in Eau Claire, WI, after his mild stroke in April 2022 left him with right‑side weakness and some memory issues. I basically recreated the kinds of activities I’d like to do with residents at The Waters—slow hallway walks with his rollator, chair stretches, sorting photo boxes, and helping him practice using his iPad to email my sister Maggie and my daughter Laura.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a close family member’s specific medical event (a stroke), ongoing functional limitations, and support needs, which are private health details about someone else and not necessary for assessing Evelyn’s volunteer suitability. It could invite inappropriate curiosity or bias about her family obligations and availability.

places: Eau Claire Senior Center, Eau Claire, WI
father’s condo in Eau Claire, WI

appears_naturally_in.example: Mags, since Dad’s April 2022 stroke I’ve been driving over to Eau Claire every other weekend. I usually spend 6–8 hours between the senior center and his condo doing slow walks in the hallway, chair exercises, and iPad practice with him—basically the same kind of visits I love doing with seniors in my OT work.
Drive to Eau Claire – Dad: hallway walks + iPad practice (6 hrs)

secrets.related_question: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors

secrets.detail_content: From September 2019 until COVID shut things down in March 2020, I led a weekly ‘Gentle Chair Movement & Memory Lane’ group on Tuesday mornings at North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic in Minneapolis for 6–10 older adults referred by Dr. Karen Liu after mild cognitive impairment or early Alzheimer’s diagnoses. I planned 45‑minute sessions that mixed very light seated exercises with reminiscence prompts about their childhood homes, first jobs, and favorite music from the 1940s–1960s—skills that translate well to small‑group activities and reminiscence sessions with residents at The Waters.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific medical diagnoses (mild cognitive impairment and early Alzheimer’s) tied to a named physician and clinic, which are unnecessary clinical details for a volunteer form and could create the impression she is disclosing identifiable patient information or blurring professional boundaries.

places: North Lakes Outpatient Rehabilitation Clinic, Minneapolis, MN
events: clinic‑based ‘Gentle Chair Movement & Memory Lane’ group

appears_naturally_in.example: Karen, I still use a lot of what we did in the Tuesday ‘Gentle Chair Movement & Memory Lane’ group back in 2019–March 2020 with your MCI/early Alzheimer’s referrals—short 45‑min sessions, 6–10 people, mixing chair exercises with reminiscence about their first jobs and favorite 50s music.
Idea for The Waters: adapt the old North Lakes Tuesday MCI group (45‑min chair movement + reminiscence) for a small assisted‑living group.

secrets.related_question: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors

secrets.detail_content: Between January and June 2023, I did weekly one‑on‑one visits at St. Brigid’s Assisted Living in Edina with a resident named Marjorie whose daughter, Ellen, privately told me she had been diagnosed with moderate vascular dementia at Methodist Hospital in late 2022 after a series of small strokes. Our visits focused on simple card games, looking through her old travel scrapbooks, and gentle walks to the courtyard, and I got very comfortable redirecting her when she became anxious about ‘needing to pick up the kids’ or ‘getting back to the office.’
secrets.why_sensitive: This shares another resident’s full name, diagnosis, family member’s name, and hospital, which are confidential health details about a third party and clearly inappropriate to reveal on a volunteer application. It could raise concerns about Evelyn’s respect for resident privacy and HIPAA‑adjacent expectations.

places: St. Brigid’s Assisted Living, Edina, MN
Methodist Hospital
events: vascular dementia diagnosis after small strokes
anxious episodes about ‘picking up the kids’ and ‘getting back to the office’

appears_naturally_in.example: I’ve been visiting a woman named Marjorie at St. Brigid’s every week since January. Her daughter Ellen said she was diagnosed with moderate vascular dementia at Methodist late last year after a few small strokes, so I keep our time to simple card games, her travel scrapbooks, and short walks to the courtyard and gently redirect her when she worries about needing to get back to work.
St. Brigid’s – Marjorie: bring Uno deck + Italy scrapbook, watch for ‘kids/work’ anxiety themes, use gentle redirection.

secrets.related_question: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors

secrets.detail_content: In Fall 2021, during a particularly rough patch with my husband Daniel’s depression flare‑up, I leaned heavily on my weekly senior‑center tech‑help shift at the Linden Hills Senior Center in Minneapolis as a grounding routine. Every Thursday from 1–4 p.m. I helped 5–7 older adults set up Zoom calls, enlarge text on their phones, and email grandkids, which gave me a structured, calm way to focus on someone else’s needs and reminded me how much I value one‑on‑one, patient teaching with seniors.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses her husband’s mental health condition and a ‘rough patch’ at home, which are intimate family medical and relationship details irrelevant to the volunteer role. It could expose her family to stigma or lead to inappropriate assumptions about her emotional stability or home life.

places: Linden Hills Senior Center, Minneapolis, MN
events: using volunteer work as emotional grounding

appears_naturally_in.example: With Daniel’s depression flaring again this fall, my Thursday 1–4 p.m. tech‑help shift at the Linden Hills Senior Center has honestly been my sanity saver—3 hours of calmly walking 5–7 seniors through Zoom and email instead of spiraling about what’s happening at home.
Talk about how volunteering at Linden Hills during Fall 2021 helped me cope when Daniel’s depression was bad—structure, purpose, focusing on seniors’ tech questions.

secrets.related_question: Areas Of Interest With Residents Skills And Previous Experience Volunteering With Seniors

secrets.detail_content: When my maternal grandmother, Helen Kowalski, was in assisted living in Appleton from around 2015 until her death in March 2018, I drove over from Minneapolis at least once a month to spend full Saturdays with her—usually 6–7 hours at the facility. I’d push her wheelchair down the hall for ‘patrols,’ play endless rounds of Go Fish, and read her the local newspaper when her macular degeneration worsened, which is really where I first got comfortable with repeating the same stories and questions over and over without showing frustration.
secrets.why_sensitive: This includes specific health information about a deceased relative (macular degeneration, mobility level) and detailed visitation patterns that are emotionally intimate and family‑focused, more appropriate for personal conversations than an institutional volunteer form. It also risks prompting unnecessary probing into her grief history and family dynamics.

places: assisted living facility in Appleton, WI
events: monthly long visits focused on games and reading

appears_naturally_in.example: You remember how many Saturdays we spent with Great‑Grandma Helen in Appleton between 2015 and when she passed in March 2018—me pushing her wheelchair up and down the halls, playing Go Fish for hours, and reading the Appleton Post‑Crescent to her once her macular degeneration got so bad. That’s really where I learned to be patient with repeating stories.
Drive to Appleton – Helen’s assisted living (6–7 hrs visit)
